plex-dump, omni-dump

plex-dump plex-name dump-tag
query
query
...

or

omni-dump omniplex-name dump-tag
query
query
...

These commands make it possible to dump the population of complexes and subcomplexes more selectively. Among the complexes whose structure is given by the plex plex-name, only those that satisfy stated queries are counted in the population dump. Since the complex structure is determined by the plex plex-name, these queries must be about the state of mols in the complex or subcomplex: is this mol phosphorylated, is that mol methylated, etc. To make the dump happen, use the dump-tag in a dump command just as you would use a species name. Behavior for omniplexes is analogous: only the population of complexes containing the given subcomplex and satisfying the queries are counted in the dump. Queries are defined and loaded in the units that define the properties tested by the queries, so they are described in their unit's documentation. See, for example, the variety of silly queries added by the phosphorylation unit for testing the phosphorylation state of mols in a complex.

Note that if you just want to dump the population of all the complexes with a given structure, you can just use the plex name in a dump command. To dump the population of all the complexes containing a certain subcomplex, use the omni-dump command without any queries to make a dump-tag that you can use in a dump command.
